message.content:
You are verifier #2, independent of all other verifiers. Do NOT confirm findings optimistically — only confirm if you can independently verify the issue exists in the actual code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay.

Finding F-007: "Gate decisions silently omit the provenance trail when recorder=None"
File: src/assay_engine/orchestration/gatenode.py (line 89)
Description: make_gate_node takes optional recorder defaulting to None. When omitted, gate decisions recorded only as LangGraph graph state, NOT hash-chained, NOT tamper-evident. The default makes the insecure configuration effortless to accidentally ship.
PoC: Call make_gate_node(gate, propose) without recorder. Run a study. Trail has no gate entries. Edit LangGraph checkpoint to flip rejected to approved; verify_records passes.

Instructions:
1. Read the actual file at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/orchestration/gatenode.py
2. Look at the code at line 89 and surrounding context
3. Independently verify whether the described problem EXISTS in the current code
4. Check if the PoC is reproducible from the actual code as written
5. Consider whether this is already fixed (pass-1 #101-#122, pass-2 #124-#156 fixed many issues)
6. Return your independent verdict

Confirmed = true ONLY if: (a) the code problem exists as described, (b) the PoC is reproducible from current code, (c) it is a genuine production-readiness concern. Set confirmed=false for speculative issues, issues already fixed, or issues where the PoC does not match what the code actually does.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

content.content:
1	"""Human-in-the-loop gate node — the interrupt/resume protocol (GOVERNANCE.md).
2	
3	:func:`make_gate_node` turns an engine :class:`~assay_engine.orchestration.gates.Gate` plus a
4	proposal builder into a LangGraph node that pauses the run at the gate (``interrupt``),
5	surfaces a proposal to the operator, and on resume validates and records the decision.
6	
7	The **gate-correlation guard** rejects a resumed decision aimed at a *different* gate than the
8	one currently parked — a stale or misrouted decision (e.g. delivered late through the
9	governance channel) must not be applied to whatever gate happens to be waiting. The guard's
10	expected gate is the gate the operator named in the resume value, compared against this node's
11	gate; auto-deriving it from the parked gate would make the guard always match and thus a no-op.
12	
13	Two further hardened behaviors, ported faithfully from the prior platform's gate node:
14	
15	- **Re-prompt on a bad resume (not raise).** ``interrupt()`` consumes and *persists* the
16	  resume value into the checkpoint *before* the node returns, so raising on a malformed or
17	  mis-correlated value would strand it in the checkpoint and replay it forever — the gate
18	  would be permanently bricked. Instead the node validates inside a loop and re-fires
19	  ``interrupt()`` (carrying the error) on a bad value, so the operator can re-submit and
20	  actually recover.
21	- **Terminal-decision idempotency.** An ``approved`` decision is terminal: re-entering an
22	  already-approved gate is a no-op (it does not re-prompt or re-record). ``rejected`` and
23	  ``deferred`` are deliberately NOT terminal, so a reject→revise→re-review loop re-fires the
24	  interrupt as intended.
25	
26	The node returns a partial state update appending one record to ``gate_decisions``; a study's
27	graph state should declare ``gate_decisions`` with a list/add reducer so records accumulate
28	across gates. The decision/recording validator (:func:`apply_gate_decision`) is pure and
29	tested offline; only the ``interrupt`` call requires LangGraph (the optional ``orchestration``
30	extra, imported lazily).
31	"""
32	
33	from __future__ import annotations
34	
35	from typing import Any, Callable, Mapping
36	
37	from assay_engine.orchestration.gates import Gate, GateError
38	
39	GateProposal = Callable[[Mapping[str, Any]], Mapping[str, Any]]
40	
41	_VALID_DECISIONS = frozenset({"approved", "rejected", "deferred"})
42	
43	
44	def apply_gate_decision(gate: Gate, resumed: Any) -> dict[str, Any]:
45	    """Validate a resumed operator decision and return the state update to record it.
46	
47	    Raises :class:`GateError` if the resume value is malformed, targets a different gate
48	    (correlation guard), or carries an unrecognized decision.
49	    """
50	    if not isinstance(resumed, Mapping):
51	        raise GateError(
52	            f"gate {gate.name!r}: resume value must be a mapping, got {type(resumed).__name__}"
53	        )
54	    target = resumed.get("gate_id")
55	    if target != gate.name:
56	        raise GateError(
57	            f"gate {gate.name!r}: resumed decision targets {target!r} — stale or misrouted; "
58	            "rejecting (gate-correlation guard)"
59	        )
60	    decision = str(resumed.get("decision", "")).strip().lower()
61	    if decision not in _VALID_DECISIONS:
62	        raise GateError(
63	            f"gate {gate.name!r}: invalid decision {decision!r} "
64	            f"(expected one of {sorted(_VALID_DECISIONS)})"
65	        )
66	    # A rejection/deferral with no rationale is governance-relevant; record a sentinel rather
67	    # than a blank (incl. whitespace-only) so the audit trail is never silently empty.
68	    rationale = str(resumed.get("rationale") or "").strip() or "No rationale provided."
69	    record = {"gate": gate.name, "decision": decision, "rationale": rationale}
70	    return {"gate_decisions": [record]}
71	
72	
73	# Only an approval is terminal: a re-entered approved gate is a no-op, but a rejected/deferred
74	# gate must re-fire its interrupt so a revise→re-review loop can re-prompt the operator.
75	_TERMINAL_DECISIONS = frozenset({"approved"})
76	
77	
78	def already_decided(state: Mapping[str, Any], gate_name: str) -> bool:
79	    """True if ``gate_name`` already holds a terminal (approved) decision in ``gate_decisions``."""
80	    for record in state.get("gate_decisions", []) or []:
81	        if record.get("gate") == gate_name and record.get("decision") in _TERMINAL_DECISIONS:
82	            return True
83	    return False
84	
85	
86	GateRecorder = Callable[[Mapping[str, Any]], None]
87	
88	
89	def make_gate_node(
90	    gate: Gate,
91	    propose: GateProposal,
92	    *,
93	    recorder: GateRecorder | None = None,
94	) -> Callable[[Mapping[str, Any]], dict[str, Any]]:
95	    """Build a LangGraph node that parks at ``gate`` for an operator decision.
96	
97	    ``propose(state)`` returns the proposal payload surfaced to the operator (what the gate is
98	    asking them to approve). The node returns a ``{"gate_decisions": [record]}`` update, or
99	    ``{}`` (no-op) if the gate is already terminally approved.
100	
101	    ``recorder`` (optional) bridges each validated decision into the hash-chained provenance
102	    trail (#111): the durable LangGraph path otherwise records gate decisions only as
103	    (untamper-evident) graph state, so the documented append-only trail would miss them. Pass a
104	    callback such as ``lambda r: trail.record("gate", f"gate {r['gate']}: {r['decision']}", **r)``
105	    so the graph path's approvals/rejections land in the same chained trail as the runner's.
106	    """
107	
108	    def node(state: Mapping[str, Any]) -> dict[str, Any]:
109	        if already_decided(state, gate.name):
110	            return {}  # terminal decision exists — do not re-prompt or re-record
111	        try:
112	            from langgraph.types import interrupt
113	        except ImportError as exc:  # pragma: no cover - exercised only without the extra
114	            raise RuntimeError(
115	                "orchestration requires the 'orchestration' extra (langgraph) — not installed"
116	            ) from exc
117	        proposal = dict(propose(state))
118	        current: dict[str, Any] = {"gate_id": gate.name, "proposal": proposal}
119	        # Validate inside a loop: on a bad/mis-correlated resume, re-fire interrupt (next
120	        # resume index) carrying the error rather than raising — a raise would strand the
121	        # bad value in the checkpoint and replay it forever, bricking the gate.
122	        while True:
123	            resumed = interrupt(current)
124	            try:
125	                update = apply_gate_decision(gate, resumed)
126	            except GateError as exc:
127	                current = {"gate_id": gate.name, "proposal": proposal, "error": str(exc)}
128	                continue
129	            if recorder is not None:
130	                for rec in update.get("gate_decisions", []):
131	                    recorder(rec)  # land the decision in the hash-chained provenance trail
132	            return update
133	
134	    return node
135

51	    attaching the durable ``checkpointer``. Returns the compiled graph.
52	
53	    Set ``requires_checkpointer=True`` for any graph containing gate nodes: ``interrupt`` is a
54	    silent no-op without a checkpointer, so a gate graph compiled without one would never park
55	    for the operator. The engine fails loud rather than ship a silently-broken governance

72	    _require_langgraph()

74	
75	    builder = StateGraph(state_schema)

81	
82	def _thread_config(run_id: str, recursion_limit: int) -> dict[str, Any]:
83	    # Resume/run are keyed ONLY by thread_id — the checkpointer loads the latest (interrupted)
84	    # state itself. We deliberately do NOT thread a checkpoint_id: passing one is the
85	    # time-travel/replay API, and Command(resume=...) WITH a checkpoint_id replays from that
86	    # historical checkpoint instead of resuming the interrupt (version-dependent; LangGraph
87	    # issue #7361) — which would re-execute already-completed gates and break governance order.
88	    return {
89	        "configurable": {"thread_id": run_id},
90	        "recursion_limit": recursion_limit,
91	    }
92	
93	
94	def run(
95	    graph: Any,
96	    state: Any,
97	    *,
98	    run_id: str,
99	    recursion_limit: int = RECURSION_LIMIT,
100	    trace: bool = True,
101	) -> Any:
102	    """Invoke a compiled graph for ``run_id`` (the checkpointer thread id), within a trace
103	    context, bounded by ``recursion_limit``. The run parks at the first gate ``interrupt``;
104	    resume it with :func:`resume`.
105	
106	    Uses ``durability="sync"``: a governance run must have its parked-at-gate checkpoint written
107	    *before* the operator is notified, so a crash in the (otherwise async) write window cannot
108	    lose the interrupt. (The LangGraph default is ``"async"``.)
109	    """
110	    if trace:
111	        bootstrap_tracing()
112	    config = _thread_config(run_id, recursion_limit)
113	    with run_trace_context(run_id):
114	        return graph.invoke(state, config=config, durability="sync")
115	
116

125	    trace: bool = True,
126	) -> Any:

128	
129	    ``gate_id`` is the gate the operator intended to decide (from the governance channel) and

content.text:
The code is clear. Let me now assess the finding fully.

The finding describes: `make_gate_node` has `recorder=None` as default, meaning gate decisions are only stored in LangGraph graph state (not hash-chained/tamper-evident) when `recorder` is omitted.

From the code at lines 89-134:
- Line 93: `recorder: GateRecorder | None = None` — confirmed, default is `None`
- Lines 129-131: The recorder is only called when `recorder is not None`
- The docstring itself (lines 101-105) explicitly acknowledges this limitation: "the durable LangGraph path otherwise records gate decisions only as (untamper-evident) graph state, so the documented append-only trail would miss them"
- The graph.py docstring (lines 61-65) also acknowledges this: "without it, the graph path's gate decisions live only as graph state"

The issue is real: calling `make_gate_node(gate, propose)` without `recorder` means gate decisions don't enter the hash-chained provenance trail. The code acknowledges this but doesn't enforce recorder as required. The PoC is reproducible — there's no guard that raises or warns when `recorder=None` at call time or at decision time.

However, I note this is documented as a known limitation with "pass a callback" guidance, rather than a silent bug. But the finding's point stands: the default makes the insecure path effortless to ship accidentally.

/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/orchestration/__init__.py:12:    make_gate_node,
/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/orchestration/__init__.py:38:    "make_gate_node",
/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/orchestration/graph.py:58:    :func:`~assay_engine.orchestration.gatenode.make_gate_node` **must pass
/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/orchestration/graph.py:63:    integrity on the durable path, pass ``make_gate_node`` a ``recorder`` that writes each

content.text: The function is exported publicly and caller-supplied — the production API allows calling it without `recorder`. No enforcement exists in the code to require it.

input.reasoning: The code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/orchestration/gatenode.py line 89-93 confirms the issue exactly as described. `make_gate_node` accepts `recorder: GateRecorder | None = None` and the recorder is only invoked at lines 129-131 when `recorder is not None`. Calling `make_gate_node(gate, propose)` without a recorder results in gate decisions being written only to LangGraph `gate_decisions` graph state — not to the hash-chained provenance trail. The docstring itself acknowledges this gap (lines 101-105: "the durable LangGraph path otherwise records gate decisions only as (untamper-evident) graph state"). The PoC is reproducible: omitting recorder means no gate entries in the chained trail, making it possible to flip a checkpoint value without trail verification catching it. This is a genuine production-readiness concern for a governance system: the documented append-only tamper-evident trail silently has gaps for gate decisions when the most-ergonomic API call pattern (omitting the optional argument) is used. There is no runtime guard, no deprecation warning, and no enforcement that recorder must be supplied for gate nodes. The issue is not fixed — it is merely documented as a known limitation.
